mission.title: Install and Configure kubeadm (Kubernetes bootstrapper)
mission.description: Kubeadm is a tool designed to simplify the process of creating and managing Kubernetes clusters. It provides best-practice paths for bootstrapping a secure and minimal viable cluster, making it ideal for users who want to set up Kubernetes quickly and efficiently.

steps.title: Install Kubeadm
steps.description:
Install kubeadm on your Linux nodes. For Ubuntu 22.04, use the following commands:
```bash
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl
curl -s https://packages.cloud.google.com/apt/doc/apt-key.gpg | sudo apt-key add -
sudo bash -c 'echo "deb https://apt.kubernetes.io/ kubernetes-xenial main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list'
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-00 kubeadm=1.31.0-00 kubectl=1.31.0-00
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubeadm kubectl
```
Note: Replace `1.31.0` with the specific version you wish to install.

steps.title: Initialize the Control Plane
steps.description:
Run the following command to initialize the Kubernetes control plane:
```bash
sudo kubeadm init --kubernetes-version=v1.31.0 --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16
```
After initialization, set up your kubeconfig:
```bash
mkdir -p $HOME/.kube
sudo cp -i /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) $HOME/.kube/config
```

steps.title: Install a Pod Network
steps.description:
Install a network add-on for your cluster. For Flannel, run:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/coreos/flannel/master/Documentation/k8s-manifests/kube-flannel.yml
```

steps.title: Join Worker Nodes
steps.description:
On each worker node, run the join command provided at the end of the `kubeadm init` output. It looks like this:
```bash
kubeadm join <control-plane-ip>:6443 --token <token> --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<hash>
```

steps.title: Verify the Cluster
steps.description:
Check the status of your cluster:
```bash
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
```

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
To enable the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler, run:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloud-provider/aws/examples/cluster-autoscaler-example.yaml
```

uninstall.title: Reset Kubeadm
uninstall.description:
To remove Kubernetes and reset the node, run:
```bash
sudo kubeadm reset
sudo rm -rf $HOME/.kube
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kubernetes Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, backup your cluster configuration and data. To upgrade from v1.30 to v1.31, run:
```bash
sudo kubeadm upgrade plan
sudo kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0
```
After upgrading, update your kubelet and kubectl:
```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-00 kubectl=1.31.0-00
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart kubelet
```
Verify the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get nodes
```

troubleshooting.title: Kubelet Not Starting
troubleshooting.description:
If kubelet fails to start, check the logs:
```bash
journalctl -u kubelet
```
Common issues include misconfigured CNI or insufficient resources.

troubleshooting.title: Control Plane Node Not Ready
troubleshooting.description:
If the control plane node shows as 'NotReady', check the status of the pods in the kube-system namespace:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
```
Look for errors in the etcd or kube-apiserver pods.

troubleshooting.title: Network Issues
troubleshooting.description:
If pods cannot communicate, ensure the network add-on is correctly installed and running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
```
Check for any errors in the network plugin.

troubleshooting.title: Token Expired
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive a token expired error when joining nodes, create a new token:
```bash
kubeadm token create --print-join-command
```

troubleshooting.title: Kubeadm Upgrade Fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the upgrade fails, check the logs for kubeadm:
```bash
journalctl -xeu kubelet
```
Ensure that your cluster is in a healthy state before attempting to upgrade.

versionNotes.changes: Improved performance and stability, new features for managing cluster resources.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for older CNI plugins.

versionNotes.changes: Enhanced security features and improved upgrade paths.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain flags in kubeadm init.

resolution.summary: A successful setup of Kubeadm results in a fully functional Kubernetes cluster with a control plane and worker nodes ready to deploy applications. You should be able to verify the cluster status and have a network add-on installed.
codeSnippets:
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl
curl -s https://packages.cloud.google.com/apt/doc/apt-key.gpg | sudo apt-key add -
sudo bash -c 'echo "deb https://apt.kubernetes.io/ kubernetes-xenial main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list'
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-00 kubeadm=1.31.0-00 kubectl=1.31.0-00
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubeadm kubectl

prerequisites.cloudCLI: optional cloud CLI tool (gcloud, aws, az, oci)
prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Linux environment with sufficient resources to run a Kubernetes cluster.
